steps.label: The Analogy
steps.body: Download Delivery is the postal service for digital goods. A game studio ships a 100GB update to 50 million players worldwide — Download Delivery ensures every player gets the complete package quickly, without the studio's servers melting.

steps.label: How It Works
steps.body:
Download Delivery is Akamai's CDN product for large file distribution (software updates, game patches, firmware, ISOs). Optimizations:

- Progressive download with resume support
- Byte-range request handling for partial updates
- Background download scheduling (off-peak pre-positioning)
- Token authentication for paid/licensed content
- NetStorage integration for origin (Akamai-managed storage)

Unlike AMD (which optimizes for streaming segments), DD optimizes for complete file delivery — different caching heuristics, timeout handling, and connection management.

steps.label: Real World
steps.body: Game publishers use Download Delivery for day-one patches (50-100GB to millions of consoles simultaneously). OS vendors push updates via DD. Enterprise software companies distribute installers. The key metric is completion rate, not time-to-first-byte: every download must finish, even on flaky connections.
"Download Delivery = large file CDN. Games, updates, firmware. Optimized for completion rate and resume, not streaming."

quiz.question: A game publisher needs to push a 90GB patch to 30 million players on launch day. Which Akamai product?

options.explain: Ion is optimized for web pages and APIs, not 90GB file downloads.

options.text: Download Delivery — optimized for large file distribution with resume support
options.explain: DD handles progressive download, resume on disconnect, and scales to millions of concurrent downloads.
AMD is for video streaming, not file downloads.
Object Storage is origin, not delivery. You need CDN in front for global scale.

quiz.question: What's the key metric for Download Delivery vs. streaming delivery?

options.explain: Important for web, less so for large downloads. Completion rate matters more.

options.text: Completion rate — every download must finish, even on unreliable connections
options.explain: A 90% downloaded game patch is useless. Resume support and connection management ensure 100% completion.
That's ABR streaming (AMD territory). Downloads are full-speed, not adaptive.
Cache hits matter, but client-side completion is the user-facing metric.
